content: Revascularization for PAD: endovascular first-line for most (angioplasty, stenting, atherectomy, drug-coated balloons)—lower perioperative morbidity than surgery. Surgical bypass (autologous vein preferred) for long-segment disease, failed endovascular, or critical limb ischemia with good surgical candidates. Critical limb ischemia (CLI): rest pain >2 weeks, tissue loss, ABI <0.40, ankle pressure <50 mmHg—requires urgent revascularization to prevent amputation. Acute limb ischemia: 6 Ps (pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis, poikilothermia); Rutherford classification guides urgency; immediate anticoagulation (heparin), emergent revascularization (catheter-directed thrombolysis, thrombectomy, bypass). Amputation indicated for nonsalvageable limb, nonambulatory patients, severe comorbidities precluding revascularization. Prognosis: claudication has benign limb prognosis (major amputation <5% at 5 years) but high CV mortality (~30% at 5 years); CLI has poor prognosis without revascularization (amputation rate 25-40% at 1 year, mortality 20-25%). Wound care, infection control, offloading essential for tissue loss.
